args_redacted_json.user_message: Please prepare ~15 Beamer slides covering this paper for an ML research audience.

result_summary_json.reasoning: The user is requesting to create a slide deck based on an already referenced paper.
result_summary_json.resolved_query: Please prepare approximately 15 Beamer slides covering the referenced paper for an machine learning research audience.

args_redacted_json.instruction: Please prepare approximately 15 Beamer slides covering the referenced paper for an machine learning research audience.

result_summary_json.contribution: Introduces FASTer, a unified autoregressive vision-language-action framework combining a high-compression learnable action tokenizer (FASTerVQ) with a block-wise decoding policy (FASTerVLA) to resolve the trade-off between reconstruction fidelity and inference efficiency.
result_summary_json.method: FASTerVQ models action chunks as single-channel images, applying DCT and L1 reconstruction losses alongside Residual Vector Quantization (RVQ) to compress actions globally. FASTerVLA uses a transformer-based VLM and a lightweight Action Expert to process multi-modal states and autoregressively generate discrete action tokens via block-wise decoding.
key_results: FASTerVQ achieves superior action reconstruction quality and high token utilization, generalizing well across both in-distribution and out-of-distribution datasets.
FASTerVLA significantly accelerates inference speed using block-wise autoregressive decoding compared to standard token-by-token generation.
The unified FASTer framework outperforms state-of-the-art VLA models in overall task performance, exhibiting strong cross-embodiment and cross-task generalization capabilities.

args_redacted_json.figure_inventory:
p0-fig-001: FASTer combines a learnable action tokenizer (FASTerVQ) and an autoregressive VLA model (FASTerVLA), achieving efficient compression, fast control, and strong performance across eight real and simulated embodiments.
p0-fig-002: FASTerVQ. (a) The raw action sequence is patchified into compact tokens based on their embodied configuration. (b) FASTerVQ applies DCT and L1 reconstruction losses and adopts RVQ, encoding actions into $N_c$ code levels; each level can be reshaped into a $C_h C_a$ tensor.
p0-fig-003: FASTerVLA. (a) The model takes RGB images, proprioceptive states, and a language instruction as inputs to a transformer–based VLM. An Action Expert autoregressively generates discrete action tokens, which a VQ decoder maps into the final continuous action sequence. (b) Codes are generated codebook–w
p0-fig-004: Model Performance Comparison
p0-fig-005: Comparison of FASTer, its non-BAR variant, and FAST across backbones on the Libero benchmark.
p0-fig-006: Evaluation results of generalization ability on VLABench. The evaluation dimensions cover generalization of language, vision, and goal and knowledge transfer.
p0-fig-007: Comparison of zero-shot performance between FASTer and baselines on Droid and Bridge datasets.
p0-fig-008: Experiment setups. Diverse embodiments, scenes, and tasks for assessing multiple capability dimensions (marked by emojis).
p0-fig-009: Different BAR block sizes, including irregular granularity, and their performance on the LIBERO environment.
p0-fig-010: Different BAR block sizes, including irregular granularity, and their performance on the LIBERO environment.
p0-fig-011: Heatmap for detailed VRR of different tokenizers across ID and OOD datasets.
p0-fig-012: Visualization of action reconstruction results from MiniVLA, VQ-VLA, Fast+, and FasterVQ on representative trajectories from the Libero, Bridge, XArm, and Widow datasets.
p0-fig-013: Visualization of our evaluation tasks.
